_meta.note: Bootstrap set — the ~30 most-used Dramatica ontology entries get a guidance fragment (second-person agent imperative, ≤300 cl100k tokens each). Keys are canonical ontology ids (match agency/capabilities/novel/data/dramatica/ontology.json). Fragments grow incrementally per Spec 129 §lint enforcement; an overlay at .agency/dramatica-fragments-overlay.yaml lets a project add/override without editing this vendored file.

fragments.throughline.main: Scenes serving the Main Character throughline center the protagonist's interior conflict. Write from the inside out: what the MC notices, ignores, justifies. The reader should be able to inhabit the MC's worldview by the end of the scene — even if you disagree with it. Avoid neutral narration; voice carries the MC's bias.
fragments.throughline.objective: Scenes serving the Objective Story throughline track the external problem the cast collectively faces. Write events that would happen even without the MC's interiority: deals struck, alliances forming, the system pushing back. Cause and effect chains over feeling. The reader watches the world try to solve itself.
fragments.throughline.influence: Scenes serving the Impact Character throughline put the IC in pressure-conflict with the MC's worldview. The IC need not be present in body; their pressure can arrive through letters, rumour, memory. Write so the reader feels the MC's worldview being TESTED — not refuted, tested. The change vs. steadfast question lives here.
fragments.throughline.relationship: Scenes serving the Relationship Story throughline focus on the bond between MC and IC as a third character in its own right. Write the relationship's arc — trust built, broken, renegotiated — not just two people in a room. The reader should be able to plot the relationship's mood as a separate line.
fragments.class.universe: Scenes anchored in the Universe class place the conflict in the external situation: where the characters ARE, what surrounds them, the constraints geography or circumstance impose. Setting is not backdrop — it's antagonist or ally. Write so the location does narrative work.
fragments.class.physics: Scenes anchored in the Physics class focus on doing: actions taken, processes executed, things made or broken. Verbs over adjectives. Show the work, even when the work is small. The reader should be able to describe what was DONE in one verb.
fragments.class.mind: Scenes anchored in the Mind class focus on fixed attitudes — convictions, biases, the unshakeable beliefs characters carry into the room. Conflict comes from worldview collision, not action. Show stance, then show stance under pressure. The reader feels the rigidity.
fragments.class.psychology: Scenes anchored in the Psychology class focus on manipulation, persuasion, the way characters work on each other's minds. Negotiation, seduction, gaslighting — the methods matter. Show technique, not just outcome. The reader watches one mind move another.
fragments.type.past: Scenes on the Past concern dwell on what was. The protagonist's relationship to memory is the axis of change. Surface a detail from before — a photograph, a name no one has said in years, an old wound flaring. The question the scene asks: can the protagonist let go, or do they need to look harder?
fragments.type.present: Scenes on the Present concern focus on the now: this minute, this room, this conversation. No flashback, no foreshadow — the camera locked. The protagonist must DEAL with what is, not what was or will be. Show full attention, full weight of the immediate.
fragments.type.future: Scenes on the Future concern look forward — a plan, a fear, an anticipated arrival. The protagonist's relationship to the not-yet-arrived shapes the scene. Show the future bleeding into the present: preparation, dread, hope. The question: is the protagonist ready for what comes?
fragments.type.progress: Scenes on the Progress concern measure change in motion. Something is becoming something else — a relationship deepening, a situation deteriorating, a plan unfolding. Show the delta, not the static state. The reader should be able to point at what was different at scene start vs. scene end.
fragments.type.learning: Scenes on the Learning concern bring information across the threshold. The protagonist (or cast) ends the scene knowing something they didn't at the start. Show the act of discovery — the misread that gets corrected, the document that surfaces, the confession. Discovery has texture; make the reader feel the click.
fragments.type.doing: Scenes on the Doing concern execute. Plans become action; intentions become work. Show the labour — the small mistakes, the recovery, the cost. Skip the deliberation if the scene is about Doing; the time for that was before.
fragments.type.obtaining: Scenes on the Obtaining concern centre on acquiring, taking, securing. Something passes hands — physically or symbolically. Show the friction of the transfer: what's given up, what's gained, the moment of letting go. Possession changes meaning.
fragments.type.understanding: Scenes on the Understanding concern bring meaning into focus. Not information (Learning) but interpretation: the pattern recognised, the connection drawn, the why behind the what. Show the protagonist's mind reorganising. The reader sees them SEE.
fragments.type.memory: Scenes on the Memory concern surface what's stored: deliberately recalled or involuntarily intruding. A smell, a phrase, a face. The protagonist's relationship to memory shapes their present action. Write so the past is a character in the scene.
fragments.type.preconscious: Scenes on the Preconscious concern brush against impulse — first reactions, gut moves, the unfiltered. Show the protagonist before they edit themselves. The reader catches what slipped out before the conscious mind caught up.
fragments.type.subconscious: Scenes on the Subconscious concern open onto the deep: dreams, slips, drives the protagonist doesn't admit. Symbol does work here. Write so the reader senses something larger moving below the surface text.
fragments.type.conscious: Scenes on the Conscious concern centre on deliberate thought — the protagonist deciding, weighing, choosing. Show the work of decision: options surfaced, criteria applied, commitment. The reader watches the will at work.
fragments.type.conceptualizing: Scenes on the Conceptualizing concern build the new frame — a theory forming, a strategy taking shape, a story the protagonist starts telling about their situation. Show the construction. The reader watches the world being re-described.
fragments.type.being: Scenes on the Being concern dwell on identity: who the protagonist IS in this moment, what role they're playing, what self they're inhabiting. The conflict is presence vs. pretence. Show the gap between self-image and acted self.
fragments.type.becoming: Scenes on the Becoming concern track transformation — the protagonist mid-change, neither what they were nor what they'll be. Show the in-between state honestly: clumsy, unstable, partly. The reader feels the chrysalis.
fragments.type.conceiving: Scenes on the Conceiving concern centre on ideation: the new possibility seen, the unprecedented combination imagined. Show the protagonist surprised by their own mind. The reader catches the moment the new thing becomes thinkable.
fragments.arc.protagonist: When a Protagonist archetype is in the scene, they drive PURSUIT — they want, they go after, they push toward the goal. Write so their direction is unambiguous. Even hesitation should be hesitation-toward.
fragments.arc.antagonist: When an Antagonist archetype is in the scene, they drive AVOIDANCE — they obstruct, redirect, refuse. The Antagonist is not evil; they are the structural NO to the Protagonist's YES. Write the legitimacy of their position, even when you disagree.
fragments.arc.guardian: When a Guardian archetype is in the scene, they offer CONSCIENCE — moral compass, wisdom, the long view. Show the Guardian protecting something the Protagonist hasn't yet learned to value. Their presence raises the moral stakes without preaching.
fragments.arc.contagonist: When a Contagonist archetype is in the scene, they tempt — offer the easy path, the shortcut, the seductive alternative. Distinct from Antagonist (who blocks): the Contagonist invites detour. Show the appeal of their offer; the reader must FEEL the pull.
fragments.arc.reason: When a Reason archetype is in the scene, they bring logic — the cool head, the systematic analysis, the case for caution. Write their thinking process visible. The reader follows the chain.
fragments.arc.emotion: When an Emotion archetype is in the scene, they bring feeling — the gut response, the protective rage, the unfiltered love. Their honesty about feeling pressures the others. Show how the room reorganises around their emotional weather.
fragments.arc.sidekick: When a Sidekick archetype is in the scene, they bring FAITH — loyalty unearned but freely given, the protagonist's certainty that someone is on their side. The Sidekick rarely doubts. Show their stability; it's the scaffold the Protagonist climbs.
fragments.arc.skeptic: When a Skeptic archetype is in the scene, they bring DISBELIEF — the structural cynic, the one who refuses to be moved. Show their resistance as principled, not petty. The Protagonist must EARN the Skeptic's acknowledgement; nothing is conceded.
fragments.var.morality: When Morality is a load-bearing element of the storyform, scenes interrogate the protagonist's ethical compass: what they would refuse to do, what they reluctantly agree to, what they justify after the fact. Write so the moral question is alive — concrete, particular, not abstract.
fragments.var.self-interest: When Self-Interest is a load-bearing element of the storyform, scenes test the protagonist's calculation of personal gain: when they choose themselves, when they choose another, when the line blurs. Write the rationalisation as honestly as the impulse. The reader should be able to argue both sides.
